1:
"And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples plucked the ears of corn, and did eat, rubbing them in their hands." The witnesses note the Sabbath grainfield, the obscure "second-first" dating, and Christ's vindication of his disciples. The fathers read the scene as the loosing of the old law. Ambrose: "in His very practice and mode of action, did the Lord begin to absolve man from the observance of the old law." Epiphanius: "the bonds of the sabbath were loosened, when the great Sabbath was come in Christ, Who made us to rest from the working of our iniquities." Bede notes the disciples' simple, austere food, and reads the bruised ears mystically of mortifying the old man.

The much-disputed phrase "second-first sabbath" (Greek deuteroproton) the witnesses canvass. Theophylact and Isidore connect it to the Passover/unleavened-bread sequence; Gill surveys several theories (a feast-sabbath, Pentecost, the reckoning toward Pentecost); Henry (with Whitby) takes it as "the first sabbath after the second day of unleavened bread, from which they reckoned the seven weeks to Pentecost." Lightfoot adds rabbinic detail on the offering of the sheaf. All agree the precise meaning is uncertain; Henry: "Blessed be God we need not be critical in this matter."

Christ's defence the witnesses read as the answer of the Lord of the Sabbath. He cites David eating the showbread (vv.3-4), lawful only for the priests, to show that "ceremonial appointments may be dispensed with in cases of necessity" (Henry), and "if God's own appointments might be thus set aside for a greater good, much more may the traditions of men." Cyril notes the inconsistency of the Pharisees, who honoured David though he broke the letter. Chrysostom marks that Christ pleads servants (David, the priests) when defending his disciples, but his Father when defending himself.

The witnesses agree: as the disciples plucked grain on the Sabbath, the Pharisees objected; and Christ vindicated them by David's example and by his own authority as Lord of the Sabbath (v.5) — the Sabbath being made for man, not man for the Sabbath. The fathers read the act as the loosing of the old law's bonds in the true Sabbath, Christ, who gives rest from sin. The obscure "second-first sabbath" the witnesses leave uncertain, agreeing it need not detain faith. The verse displays the clash between the Pharisees' rigid traditions and Christ's authority: works of necessity are lawful on the Sabbath, and ceremonial appointments yield to mercy and need. The Lord of the Sabbath, not its slave, sets his disciples free.
2:
"And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?" The witnesses note the Pharisees' objection and its root in a rigid, traditionary religion. The eastern Cyril of Alexandria reads the dispute through the new-covenant lens: "no man puts new wine into old skins. The heart of the Jews then is an old skin, and therefore cannot hold the new wine... If the new covenant is the second, and different from the first, there is every necessity for those who wish to live according to it to abandon the old laws, and conform to the newness of the gospel polity." He notes the Pharisees "being altogether destitute of knowledge of the sacred Scriptures, had but one sole purpose, on every occasion to find fault with the divine and heavenly preaching."

The grammatical voices note the discrepancy among the Gospels as to whom the objection was addressed. Gill: Matthew and Mark say it was said to Jesus; "no doubt but they said it to both, first to one, and then to the other." Wesley: "It was afterward, probably, they proposed it to his disciples." The Pharisees, watching for fault, charged the disciples with reaping (rubbing the ears) on the Sabbath — a violation not of the law itself but of their traditional fence around it.

The objection the witnesses read as the carping of an envious, fault-finding spirit. Cyril of Alexandria turns the charge back: "dost not thou thyself, when setting thy sabbath meal, break the bread? Why then dost thou blame others?" The Pharisees, ignorant of the Scriptures' true intent, made the Sabbath a burden rather than a gift, and sought occasion against Christ rather than instruction from him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees, watching for fault, objected that the disciples did what was "not lawful" on the Sabbath — a charge resting not on the law itself but on their traditional additions to it. Cyril of Alexandria reads the dispute as the clash of the old covenant (the old skin that cannot hold the new wine) with the gospel's newness; the Pharisees, ignorant of the Scriptures, sought only to find fault. The Gospels differ as to whether the charge was made to Jesus or the disciples — likely to both. The verse displays the carping spirit that turns God's gracious Sabbath into a snare, and prepares for Christ's vindication of his disciples (vv.3-5). The fault-finders, blind to the Sabbath's merciful intent, accuse the innocent; and the Lord of the Sabbath will answer them from the Scriptures they professed to honour.
3:
"And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungred, and they which were with him?" The witnesses note Christ's appeal to David's example as the first of his defences. Gill: Christ "at once took up the cause of his disciples, and vindicated them, by observing what David did, when he and his men were an hungry; how that he went into the tabernacle, and took the showbread, and ate of it, and gave it to his men, who also ate of it; which, according to the law, was only allowed to priests." He adds Christ's other instance — the priests profaning the Sabbath by their temple labours and yet being blameless.

The grammatical voices note Luke's distinctive touches. Robertson: "Not even this only in Luke" — Christ's pointed "Have ye not read so much as this?" rebukes the Pharisees' professed scriptural learning. The appeal to David (1 Samuel 21:6) was an "overwhelming and crushing reply to these pettifogging ceremonialists" (Robertson, under v.4), since they honoured David as saint and prophet though he had broken the letter of the law in his need.

The argument the witnesses (with Cyril, under v.1) read as exposing the Pharisees' inconsistency: they blamed the disciples for what they excused in David. The law of necessity, recognized in David's case, justified the disciples; and the God who allowed the showbread to feed the hungry king would not condemn the hungry followers of David's greater Son. The example, drawn from a passage the Pharisees revered, silenced their objection from their own Scriptures.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ answers the Pharisees by David's example (1 Samuel 21:6) — David, hungry, ate the showbread lawful only for the priests, and was not condemned. The pointed "Have ye not read so much as this?" rebukes the fault-finders' professed learning; and the appeal, drawn from a passage they revered, exposes their inconsistency in honouring David while blaming the disciples for the like necessity. The law of need, recognized in David's case, justifies the hungry followers of David's greater Son. The verse opens Christ's crushing reply from the Pharisees' own Scriptures: the letter yields to mercy and necessity, and the God who fed the hungry king will not condemn the hungry disciples. The Lord of the Sabbath argues not from his bare authority alone but from the Scriptures the cavillers claimed to honour, leaving them no answer.
4:
"How he went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone?" The witnesses note the completion of the David argument and a famous textual curiosity. On the argument, Robertson lists Christ's five defences across the Synoptics: "the example of David, the work of the priests on the sabbath, the prophecy of Hosea 6:6, the purpose of the sabbath for man, the Son of Man lord of the sabbath." The showbread, eaten by David and his men though reserved for the priests (Leviticus 24:9), proves that the letter of ceremonial law yields to necessity.

A noted textual addition follows this verse in Codex Bezae. Clarke records it: "On the same day, seeing one working on the Sabbath, he said unto him, Man, if indeed thou knowest what thou dost, blessed art thou; but if thou knowest not, thou art cursed, and art a transgressor of the law." He reads its meaning: to work on the Sabbath from conviction that it is abolished and a new one instituted is blessed (instructed in the Messiah's kingdom); but to do so from contempt of God's law is accursed. Robertson notes Codex D's transposition of v.5 to after v.10 and this insertion here — a textual curiosity, not part of the received text.

The argument the witnesses read as decisive. The showbread, the holiest of the consecrated bread, was given to David in his need; how much more might the disciples of the Messiah relieve their hunger with mere ears of corn? The law's ceremonial provisions, the witnesses note, were never meant to override mercy and necessity.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ completes the David argument — the showbread, lawful only for the priests (Leviticus 24:9), was eaten by David and his men in need without condemnation — one of five defences (Robertson) showing the ceremonial letter yields to necessity. The verse is also noted for the striking Codex Bezae addition (the man working on the Sabbath, "blessed... if thou knowest what thou dost; cursed... if thou knowest not"), a textual curiosity reflecting an early reading on Sabbath observance under the new covenant, not part of the received text (Clarke, Robertson). The argument is decisive: if the holiest consecrated bread fed the hungry king, the disciples of David's greater Son may surely relieve their hunger with ears of corn. The verse displays the subordination of ceremonial law to mercy and need, and preserves a glimpse of the early Church's reflection on the Sabbath in the light of Christ.
5:
"And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath." The witnesses note the climactic claim of Christ's lordship over the Sabbath. Theophylact reads it as the Lawgiver's prerogative: "I am the Lord of the sabbath, as being He who ordained it, and as the Legislator I have power to loose the sabbath; for Christ was called the Son of man, who being the Son of God yet condescended in a miraculous manner to be made and called for man's sake the Son of man." Chrysostom adds Mark's complementary word: "The sabbath was made for man, not man for the sabbath. It is therefore more fitting that the sabbath should be subject to man, than that man should bow his neck to the sabbath."

The claim the witnesses (with Henry, under v.1) read as both the seal of Christ's defence and the foundation of the Lord's Day. Henry: "In the kingdom of the Redeemer, the sabbath day is to be turned into a Lord's day... it is to be observed chiefly in honour of the Redeemer, as it had been before in honour of the Creator." Christ, as Lord of the Sabbath, has authority both to interpret its true meaning (mercy and necessity, not rigid abstinence) and to transfer its observance to the day of his resurrection.

The title "Son of man" the witnesses note as the very name under which Christ claims this lordship — the Son of God made man for man's sake, who therefore has authority over the institution made for man. The Sabbath is his servant, not his master; and he frees it from the Pharisees' bondage to serve its true end.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, the climax of the Sabbath controversy. The witnesses agree: Christ seals his defence with the claim of his lordship over the Sabbath — as its ordainer and Legislator (Theophylact), he has authority to interpret and to loose it. The complementary word "the sabbath was made for man" (Chrysostom) shows the Sabbath is man's servant, not his master, made for his benefit. The title "Son of man" — the Son of God become man for man's sake — is the very name under which Christ claims this authority. The verse both crowns the vindication of the disciples and lays the foundation of the Lord's Day: Christ, Lord of the Sabbath, frees it from the Pharisees' bondage to serve its merciful end, and in his kingdom transforms it into the day of resurrection joy. The Sabbath finds its Lord and its true meaning in him.
6:
"And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered." The witnesses note the second Sabbath controversy and the healing of the withered hand. The fathers read the setting and the symbol. Bede: "He chiefly heals and teaches on the sabbaths, not only to convey the meaning of a spiritual sabbath, but because of the more numerous assembly of the people." Cyril notes the Pharisees' malice: "this is the way of the envious man, he feeds in himself his pang of grief with the praises of others." Bede reads the withered hand of the human race "withered by the unfruitfulness of good works, because of the hand in our first parent stretched forth to take the apple, which was healed by the innocent hand stretched forth on the cross." Ambrose applies it morally: "beware lest your hand be contracted by avarice or sacrilege. Stretch it forth oftener to help your neighbour... to the poor man who beseeches you."

The grammatical voices note Luke's distinctive precision. Robertson: "Right hand. This alone in Luke, the physician's eye for particulars." He asks whether Luke, as a physician, "had to meet this problem in his own practice." Ellicott: "St. Luke alone specifies which hand it was that was affected." Clarke rebuts the rationalist who would make it a dislocated joint cured without miracle: "deserves no serious refutation."

The healing on the Sabbath the witnesses read as Christ's deliberate vindication of doing good. Cyril (under the scene): the law was given for mercy — God commanded the Sabbath rest that the servant and the cattle might rest; "he who has mercy on his ox, how much rather will he not have mercy on man troubled with a severe disease?" Augustine harmonizes Matthew (they asked) with Luke (they were asked): both happened.

The witnesses agree: on another Sabbath, in the synagogue, Christ healed a man with a withered right hand — Luke alone, with the physician's eye, noting which hand. The fathers read the withered hand of humanity, unfruitful in good works since the fall, healed by the hand stretched on the cross, and draw the moral lesson of a hand stretched out in charity. Christ heals deliberately on the Sabbath to vindicate doing good, the Sabbath law itself being given for mercy. The verse opens the second Sabbath controversy: the Pharisees' envious watching against Christ's merciful power. The withered hand restored proclaims that the Sabbath is for healing and good, not for the rigid abstinence the Pharisees demanded even from works of mercy.
7:
"And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him." The witnesses note the malicious watching of Christ's enemies. The grammatical voices stress the word "watched." Clarke: "They maliciously watched him. This is the import of the word." Robertson: "Imperfect middle, were watching for themselves on the side... watching either assiduously like the physician at the bedside or insidiously with evil intent as here. They were determined to make a case against Jesus."

Bede (under v.6) had noted the trap: "their object is now by watching to bring a false accusation against the Master Himself... that if He did not heal, they might accuse Him of cruelty or impotence; if He did, of violation of the sabbath." The Pharisees set a snare in which, whichever way Christ acted, they hoped to accuse him.

The watching the witnesses read as the mark of the envious, fault-finding spirit. Cyril (under v.6): "such is the nature of an envious man, that he makes the praises of others food for his own disease, and wickedly is maddened by their reputation." The scribes and Pharisees, rather than learning from Christ's teaching or rejoicing in his healing, lay in wait to entrap him — a spirit that turns even the good into occasion for accusation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the scribes and Pharisees maliciously watched Christ, hoping he would heal on the Sabbath that they might accuse him — a snare in which, healing or not, they sought to entrap him (cruelty if he refused, Sabbath-breaking if he healed). The word "watched" (Robertson, Clarke) marks the insidious, evil intent, the watching of spies rather than learners. The verse displays the envious, fault-finding spirit that makes the good itself an occasion for accusation: the Pharisees, blind to the mercy before them, lie in wait against the Healer. Their malicious watching, far from hindering Christ, only sets the stage for his deliberate vindication of doing good on the Sabbath (vv.8-10), and reveals the hardness of heart that the miracle would only inflame to madness (v.11). The watchers for fault become witnesses against themselves.
8:
"But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth." The witnesses note Christ's knowledge of his enemies' hearts and his open, deliberate action. On his knowledge, Barnes: "He knew their thoughts — their dark, malicious designs — by the question which they proposed to him." Robertson: "Jesus, in contrast to these spies, read their intellectual processes like an open book." The omniscience of Christ, the witnesses note, discerned the malice they had not openly spoken.

The Reformed Calvin harmonizes the accounts: "If Matthew states the truth, they had openly declared by their language what was in their minds... But both may be true, that they spoke plainly, and yet that Christ discerned their secret designs; for they did not openly avow their designs... Luke means nothing more than that Christ was aware of their insidious designs, though not expressed in words."

Christ's command — "Rise up, and stand forth in the midst" — the witnesses read as the open, fearless working of the miracle before all. Robertson: "Christ worked right out in the open where all could see. It was a moment of excitement when the man stepped forth there before them all." Cyril (under v.6) suggests the man was set forth "that perchance he might stir up the cruel Pharisees to pity, and allay the flames of their passion."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, knowing the malicious thoughts of his enemies (his omniscience reading their hearts like an open book, Robertson), commanded the man with the withered hand to stand forth in the midst — working the miracle openly, fearlessly, before all. Calvin harmonizes the accounts: whether or not they spoke their malice aloud (Matthew), Christ discerned their secret design (Luke). The setting of the man in the midst, the fathers suggest, was meant to move the Pharisees to pity and quench their envy. The verse displays Christ's fearless openness against his enemies' insidious watching: he does not shrink from doing good before their hostile eyes but sets the man forth where all may see. The Healer who knows their hearts will not be deterred by their malice; and the public miracle, far from hiding, declares his power and his mercy in the face of accusation.
9:
"Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?" The witnesses note Christ's penetrating counter-question. Robertson: "He now asks in addition an open question that brings the whole issue into the open." The question, Cyril (under v.6) noted, was "very useful": if it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath, cease accusing Christ; if it is not, you become the accuser of the law itself, which was given for mercy.

The grammatical voices note the sharp edge of "to save life, or to destroy it." Robertson: "On this very day these Pharisees were plotting to destroy Jesus (verse 7). The words of Jesus cut to the quick." Wesley: "He just then probably saw the design to kill him rising in their hearts." The contrast is pointed: Christ purposes to save (heal) on the Sabbath, while his accusers purpose to destroy (kill) on the very same day — turning the question back upon their own murderous intent.

The question the witnesses read as exposing the absurdity of the Pharisees' position. To omit to do good, Barnes notes, "was, in fact, doing evil." There is no neutral ground: the Sabbath, given for mercy, cannot forbid the saving of life; and those who would forbid healing on it make the day of rest a day of cruelty, while themselves plotting murder.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ silences his accusers with a penetrating question — is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or evil, to save life or destroy it? The question brings the whole issue into the open (Robertson) and exposes the absurdity of forbidding mercy on the day given for mercy; to omit good is itself to do evil (Barnes). The sharp contrast "save or destroy" cuts to the quick: Christ purposes to save (heal) on the Sabbath, while his accusers purpose to destroy (kill) him on the very same day (v.7) — turning their own murderous intent against them. The verse displays Christ's mastery in controversy: a single question dismantles the Pharisees' position, for the Sabbath cannot be made an excuse for refusing to do good or save life. The Healer who would save exposes the accusers who would destroy, and leaves them no answer.
10:
"And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other." The witnesses note the look, the command, and the complete cure. On the look, Robertson: "First aorist middle participle as in Mark 3:5, the middle voice giving a personal touch... Mark adds 'with anger' which Luke here does not put in." Christ looked round on them all — searching, grieved at their hardness — before working the miracle openly.

The command "Stretch forth thy hand" the witnesses note is identical in all three Gospels. The man's obedience to the seemingly impossible command was itself the means of the cure: in attempting to stretch out the withered hand, he found it restored. Augustine (under v.6) records the word of Titus Bostrensis: "I command you, Who created man," and the man "hears, and is made whole."

The complete restoration the witnesses stress. Robertson: the verb "means complete restoration to the former state." Clarke rebuts the rationalist again: even were it a dislocated joint, natural healing "would have required several weeks to restore the muscles and ligaments... Why all this learned labour to leave God out of the question?" The hand was made "whole as the other" — a full, instantaneous miracle.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, looking round on them all (grieved, Mark adds, at their hardness), commanded the man to stretch forth his hand, and it was restored whole as the other — a complete, instantaneous cure. The man's obedience to the impossible command was the means of his healing: stretching out the withered hand, he found it whole. The grammatical voices stress the completeness of the restoration and rebut the rationalist who would explain away the miracle (Clarke). The verse displays Christ's open vindication of doing good on the Sabbath: in full view of his hostile watchers, he heals by a word, and the withered hand is made whole. The look round on them all, the searching command, the instant cure — all proclaim the divine power and mercy of the Lord of the Sabbath, who does good openly in the face of those who plot his destruction. The miracle answers the question of v.9 beyond all dispute.
11:
"And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus." The witnesses note the enemies' rage and conspiracy. On the "madness," Robertson: "Here is rage that is kin to insanity, for anoias is lack of sense. An old word, but only here and 2 Timothy 3:9 in the N.T." Barnes lists the causes: Christ had shown his power, shown it contrary to their views, shown himself from God, and shown no respect to their traditions — "pride, obstinacy, malice, and disappointed self-confidence" combined to produce madness.

The grammatical voices note the conspiracy. Robertson: "Mark 3:6 notes that they bolted out of the synagogue and outside plotted even with the Herodians how to destroy Jesus, strange co-conspirators these against the common enemy... Already nearly two years before the end we see the set determination to destroy Jesus." Ellicott notes Luke, perhaps from his acquaintance with people connected with the Herods, omits the Herodians' part that Mark records.

The rage the witnesses read as the fruit of envy and hardness. Barnes: "Men are often enraged because others do good in a way which they do not approve of... because he has not accommodated himself to their views of what is right." Clarke (citing Quesnel): "Pride, obstinacy, and interest, combined together, are capable of any thing. When men have once framed their conscience according to their passions, madness passes for zeal." The miracle that should have moved them to faith only inflamed them to murderous fury.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the Sabbath controversy. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees, instead of being convinced by the miracle, were filled with madness — rage kin to insanity (Robertson) — and conspired (with the Herodians, Mark adds) how to destroy Jesus. Barnes traces the causes: Christ had shown his power, contrary to their views, proving himself from God and disregarding their traditions; "pride, obstinacy, malice, and disappointed self-confidence" combined to madness. The verse displays the terrible hardness of heart that turns the evidence of divine goodness into occasion for murderous fury: the good itself enrages those whose consciences are framed by their passions (Clarke). The healing that should have led them to faith only set their determination to destroy Christ — a determination already firm nearly two years before the end. The Sabbath controversy ends not in the Pharisees' conversion but in their conspiracy: the Healer's mercy answered with murderous rage.
12:
"And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God." The witnesses note Christ's night of prayer before choosing the Twelve. The fathers read the prayer as example, not need. Ambrose: "Let not your ears be open to deceit, that you should think that the Son of God prays from want of strength... for being Himself the Author of power, He leads us by His own example to the precepts of virtue." The Gloss: "When adversaries rose up against the miracles of Christ, He chose Apostles as defenders and witnesses of the truth, and prefaces their election with prayer." Chrysostom exhorts to night prayer: "fire does not so destroy the rust of iron as nightly prayer the blight of sin."

The grammatical voices weigh the phrase "in prayer to God" (Greek possibly "the prayer of God"). Lightfoot notes the rabbinic notion that God prays "by way of authority," and leaves the phrase undetermined. Most take it simply as Christ's prayer to God. Henry draws the example of secret prayer: "He continued a whole night in meditation and secret prayer. We have a great deal of business at the throne of grace."

The night of prayer the witnesses connect to the weight of the coming choice. Henry: "after he had continued all night in prayer, one would have thought that, when it was day, he should have reposed himself... No, as soon as anybody was stirring, he called unto him his disciples. Ministers are to be ordained with prayer more than ordinarily solemn." The choosing of the Twelve, on whom the Church would be built, was prefaced by Christ's whole night of prayer.

The witnesses agree: before choosing the Twelve, Christ went up into a mountain and continued all night in prayer to God — an example to us, not a sign of weakness, for he is himself the Author of power (Ambrose). The fathers exhort us by his example to secret, persevering, even nightly prayer, which destroys the rust of sin (Chrysostom). The weight of the coming choice — the apostles on whom the Church would rest — moved Christ to so solemn a vigil, teaching that great undertakings, and especially the ordination of ministers, should be prefaced by earnest prayer (Henry). The verse displays the Lord's own dependence, in his human nature, on prayer: he who needed nothing prayed all night for us, that we might learn to seek God before every great work. The choosing of the Twelve flows from the night of prayer, and the Church is founded in the secret communion of the Son with the Father.
13:
"And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles." The witnesses note the choosing of the Twelve from the larger body and the giving of the name "apostles." Clarke: "He chose twelve Out of them. Our Lord at this time had several disciples... and out of these converts he chose twelve, whom he appointed to the work of the ministry; and called them apostles, i.e. persons sent or commissioned by himself." Robertson: "He chose (of himself, and for himself, indirect middle voice). It was a crisis in the work of Christ. Jesus assumed full responsibility even for the choice of Judas."

On "named apostles," the grammatical voices note its meaning and weight. Robertson: "apostle is missionary, one sent... these twelve apostles stand apart from all others in that they were all chosen at once by Jesus himself that they might be with him (Mark 3:14), to be trained by Jesus himself... In the nature of the case they could have no successors as they had to be personal witnesses to the life and resurrection of Jesus (Acts 1:22)." Calvin notes the name was given "with a view to their future rank... to inform them why they were separated from the ordinary class, and for what purpose they were destined."

Ellicott notes the timing: Luke (with Mark) distinguishes the choice of the Twelve from their later mission (Luke 9:1), and places it "as the result of the night of prayer." The choosing flows directly from Christ's vigil (v.12); the Church's foundation is laid in prayer.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after his night of prayer, Christ called his disciples and chose twelve from among them, naming them "apostles" — those sent, commissioned by himself. He chose them himself, assuming full responsibility even for Judas (Robertson); the name marked their separation for a future rank and office (Calvin). The apostles stand apart from all others: chosen at once by Christ, trained by him, to be personal witnesses to his life and resurrection (Acts 1:22), and so without successors in that unique office. The choosing flows directly from the night of prayer (v.12), the Church's foundation laid in Christ's communion with the Father. The verse marks the constituting of the apostolic college — the twelve men through whom Christ would build his Church and bring his gospel to the world — chosen deliberately, named significantly, and set apart by the praying Lord.
14:
"Simon, (whom he also named Peter,) and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew." The witnesses note the first names in the apostolic catalogue. On Simon Peter, Gill: "Peter, which signifies a rock, or stone, as Cephas also does... from his constancy, steadfastness, and solidity." Bede (under v.12) noted the surname was given long before, at his first coming (John 1:42), but Luke marks here that "this was not his name before, but the Lord had given it to him."

The fathers (under v.12) note the order and the men. Eusebius: James and John, sons of Zebedee, fishermen; Philip of Bethsaida, the city of Andrew and Peter; Bartholomew "a simple man, devoid of all worldly knowledge and guile." Ambrose draws the lesson of their lowliness: "Not the wise men, not the rich, not the noble, but He chose to send out fishermen and publicans, that they might not seem to turn men to their grace by riches or by the influence of power... and that the force of truth, not the graces of oratory, might prevail."

The catalogue the witnesses note is given by name, Cyril observed (under v.12), "that no one should dare to insert any others in the catalogue." The naming of each apostle fixes the apostolic foundation: these specific men, chosen by Christ, are the witnesses and pillars of the Church.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostolic catalogue begins with Simon (named Peter, the rock, for his steadfastness, Gill) and his brother Andrew, James and John (sons of Zebedee), Philip and Bartholomew — humble men, fishermen and the like, chosen not for wealth or learning but that the force of truth, not oratory, might prevail (Ambrose). The Lord's surnaming of Simon as Peter, given at his first coming (John 1:42) and marked here, signals the new identity Christ confers. The naming of each apostle by name (Cyril) fixes the apostolic foundation: these specific men, chosen by the praying Lord, are the witnesses and pillars of the Church. The verse begins the roll of the Twelve — the lowly men through whom Christ would conquer the world by truth, a roll that displays the grace of God in choosing the weak and obscure to confound the mighty. The foundation of the Church is named, man by man.
15:
"Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes." The witnesses note the next names in the apostolic roll. On Matthew and Thomas, Gill: Matthew "was a publican, and who also was called Levi; and the latter had besides the name of Didymus, and was he that was so unbelieving of Christ's resurrection." Bede (under v.12) noted Matthew's humility: "Matthew places himself after his fellow-disciple Thomas, from humility, whereas by the other Evangelists he is put before him."

On Simon Zelotes, the grammatical voices explain the surname. Clarke: the "Zealots" were a party "who pretended to be more than ordinarily zealous for religion"; but Bishop Pearce notes that the murderous Zealots of Josephus's day were forty years later than Christ's choosing, and the name first marked "men who were more zealous for the cause of pure and undefiled religion than the rest of their neighbours." Wesley: "Simon called Zelotes — Full of zeal; otherwise called Simon the Canaanite." The Gloss (under v.12): he was of Cana in Galilee, "which is interpreted zeal."

The catalogue the witnesses note continues to display the variety of the chosen: a publican (Matthew), a doubter (Thomas), a zealot (Simon) — men of differing temper and background, united by Christ's call. The diversity of the Twelve, the witnesses note, shows the breadth of the grace that gathered them.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the roll continues with Matthew (the publican, also called Levi), Thomas (Didymus, later the doubter of the resurrection), James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes (the zealous, of Cana). Matthew's placing himself after Thomas marks his humility (Bede); Simon's surname "Zelotes" denotes his zeal for pure religion, not the later murderous party of Josephus's day (Clarke). The catalogue displays the variety of the chosen — a publican, a doubter, a zealot — men of differing temper and background, united only by Christ's call. The verse continues the apostolic roll, showing the breadth of the grace that gathered such diverse men into one company: from the tax-collector's booth and the zealot's fervour alike, Christ drew his witnesses. The foundation of the Church is laid not in uniform men but in the varied vessels of God's choosing, each named and known by the Lord who called them.
16:
"And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor." The witnesses note the last names — Jude and the traitor. On Judas the brother of James, Gill: "this Judas was also called Thaddaeus and Lebbaeus, and is the writer of the epistle that bears his name." Henry (under v.12) noted the varying order of the lists "to teach both ministers and Christians not to be nice in precedency."

On Judas Iscariot, the grammatical voices note the tragic note. Robertson: "Who became traitor, more exactly. He gave no signs of treachery when chosen." Augustine (under v.12) drew the lesson: "Judas the traitor is chosen, not unwittingly but knowingly, for Christ had indeed taken to Himself the weakness of man... He was willing to be betrayed by His own Apostle, that you when betrayed by your friend may bear calmly your mistaken judgment, your kindness thrown away."

The inclusion of the traitor in the apostolic college the witnesses read as both mystery and comfort. Christ, knowing all, chose Judas knowingly — not deceived in him (Henry, under v.12) — that those betrayed by friends might bear it patiently after his example. The presence of one false apostle among the Twelve warns that even the highest office is no guarantee of grace.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, which closes the apostolic roll. The witnesses agree: the catalogue ends with Judas the brother of James (Jude, also called Thaddaeus, writer of the epistle) and Judas Iscariot, "which also was the traitor." Robertson notes the tragic phrase: he "became" traitor, giving no sign of treachery when chosen. Augustine draws the comfort: Christ chose Judas knowingly, not deceived, willing to be betrayed by his own apostle, that those betrayed by friends might bear it calmly after his example. The presence of one false apostle among the Twelve is both mystery and warning: the highest office and nearest place to Christ are no guarantee of grace. The verse closes the roll of the Twelve with the dark note of the traitor — a sobering reminder that to be numbered among Christ's chosen, even as an apostle, is not the same as to be saved. The Lord who knew Judas's heart chose him still, for our instruction and his own purpose.
17:
"And he came down with them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases." The witnesses note the setting of the Sermon and debate its relation to the Sermon on the Mount. On the multitude, Cyril (of Alexandria) notes the Gentiles present: those "from the sea-coast of Tyre and Sidon... were idolaters," and the election of apostles was held publicly before all, "teachers appointed for all beneath the heaven." Bede reads the descent: "you will scarcely find any where that the multitudes follow our Lord to the higher places, or that a sick person is healed on a mountain... the touch of the Saviour is the work of salvation, whom to touch is to believe on Him."

The grammatical voices address the harmonistic question — is this the Sermon on the Mount? Barnes lists the resemblances (same beginning and close, same substance, both followed by the centurion's servant) and the differences (mount vs plain, sat vs stood), concluding Jesus came down to the plain, healed, then "retired into the mountain again, or to an eminence." Robertson is emphatic: "There is not the slightest reason for making Matthew locate this sermon on the mountain and Luke in the valley as if the places, audiences, and topics were different... He stopped his descent at this level place and then found a slight elevation on the mountain side." Ellicott notes the "level place" (no article: "a plain") may be on the mountain side.

On "to be healed," the witnesses note Luke's medical interest. The whole multitude sought healing both of body and soul (Theophylact). The Sermon's setting — Christ descending to the plain, healing the sick, then teaching — displays the union of his works of mercy and his words of grace.

The witnesses agree: Christ came down with the Twelve and stood on a level place, surrounded by his disciples and a great multitude from Judea, Jerusalem, and even Gentile Tyre and Sidon, who came to hear and be healed. The fathers note the descent to the lowly, where the sick are found and healed by his touch (faith). On the harmonistic question, the witnesses (especially Robertson) hold that the Sermon on the Plain and the Sermon on the Mount are one — the "level place" being on the mountain side, Christ descending then sitting to teach. The verse displays the union of Christ's healing and teaching: he descends to the multitude, heals their diseases, and then delivers the great sermon. The presence of Gentiles foreshadows the gospel's reach to all nations; and the throng seeking his touch shows the power that went out from him to save both body and soul.
18:
"And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed." The brief verse continues the description of Christ's healing ministry before the Sermon. Barnes explains "vexed": "to afflict, to torment — denoting deep and heavy trials. Unclean spirits — Demons that were impure and unholy, having a delight in tormenting." Gill: "Were possessed with devils, and sadly tormented and afflicted by them."

The grammatical voices note Luke's two verbs for healing. Robertson: "Note here both verbs, iaomai and therapeuo, used of the miraculous cures of Jesus. Therapeuo is the verb more commonly employed of regular professional cures, but no such distinction is made here." The healings, he notes, "were repeated as often as they came" — a continual outflow of healing power.

The deliverance of the demon-possessed the witnesses read as part of Christ's saving work over both disease and the powers of darkness. Cyril of Jerusalem (the lone father here) connects it to the apostolic power that would follow: "the multitude also of the cities round about came, bringing sick folk, and them that were vexed with unclean spirits, and they were healed every one in this power of the Holy Ghost." The power that healed through Christ would continue through his apostles.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: among the multitude, those vexed (tormented) by unclean spirits were healed — Christ's saving power extending over both disease and the demonic. The two verbs for healing (iaomai, therapeuo) are used without distinction (Robertson), and the cures were continual, repeated as the afflicted came. The deliverance of the demon-possessed displays Christ's authority over the powers of darkness, part of the saving work that the apostles would continue in the power of the Spirit (Cyril of Jerusalem). The verse, brief and quiet, records the breadth of Christ's healing before the Sermon: not only diseases of the body but the torments of unclean spirits yield to his power. The Healer who would teach the great sermon first displays his authority over every affliction, body and soul, that the multitude might know the power of the one who spoke. The same power would flow through his apostles to all the cities round about.
19:
"And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all." The witnesses note the healing power that went forth from Christ. Gill: the sick "were persuaded, and they found it true by experience, that if they could but touch any part of his body, or his garments, they should be cured." On "virtue" (power), Ellicott marks Luke's medical vocabulary: "The use of the term virtue (or power) in this technical sense is peculiar to St. Luke, and may be noted as characteristic of the medical Evangelist."

The grammatical voices note the continual outflow. Robertson: "Imperfect middle, power was coming out from him. This is the reason for the continual approach to Jesus... Was healing all, kept on healing all." Cyril of Alexandria (under v.17) stressed the source: "Christ did not receive virtue from others, but since He was by nature God, sending out His own virtue upon the sick, He healed them all" — the power his own, not borrowed.

Robertson draws a homiletical application: "The preacher today who is not a vehicle of power from Christ to men may well question why that is true... It was at a time of surpassing dynamic spiritual energy when Jesus delivered this greatest of all sermons. The very air was electric with spiritual power." The healing virtue going out from Christ was the divine power of the Son, healing all who touched him in faith.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the Sermon's setting. The witnesses agree: the whole multitude sought to touch Christ, for healing power (Luke's medical term "virtue," Ellicott) went forth from him and healed them all — a continual outflow (Robertson), the power his own as God by nature, not borrowed (Cyril of Alexandria). The verse displays the divine power of the Son, healing all who approached in faith; to touch him was to believe and be healed (Bede, under v.17). The "very air was electric with spiritual power" (Robertson) as Christ, surrounded by the healed multitude, prepared to deliver the great sermon. The verse closes the setting with the throng pressing to touch the Healer from whom power flowed — a vivid picture of the abundance of grace in Christ, who heals all and turns now to teach. The power that went out to heal bodies would, in the Sermon, go forth in words to heal souls; and the same Christ is the source of both.
20:
"And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the opening Beatitude and its distinctively Lukan form. The fathers address the difference from Matthew's "poor in spirit." Cyril: "Luke says, Blessed are the poor, without the addition of spirit, calling those poor who despise riches. For it became those who were to preach the Gospel to have no covetousness." Basil guards it: "not every one oppressed with poverty is blessed, but he who has preferred the commandment of Christ to worldly riches... nothing involuntary deserves a blessing." Ambrose harmonizes the two Evangelists: "Luke mentions only four blessings, but Matthew eight; but in those eight are contained these four... the one has embraced the four cardinal virtues, the other has revealed the mystical number" — and both place poverty first, "the first in order, and the purest of the virtues."

The grammatical voices note Luke's directness. Henry: "When he had laid it down for a truth, Blessed are the poor in spirit, he added, Blessed are ye poor. All believers, that take the precepts of the gospel to themselves... may take the promises to themselves." The blessing is applied directly to the disciples, who had left all to follow Christ: "you are blessed in your poverty... for yours is the kingdom of God."

On "lifted up his eyes," Ambrose: "What is lifting up the eyes, but to disclose a more hidden light?" Bede: "especially He lifts up His eyes on His disciples, that to those who receive the word listening attentively, He might reveal more fully the light of its deep meaning."

The witnesses agree: Christ opens the Sermon by blessing the poor — Luke's form ("ye poor," not Matthew's "poor in spirit") pointing to the actual poor who despise riches, yet (Basil) only those whose poverty is joined to the love of Christ's commandment, not mere involuntary want. The fathers harmonize the two Evangelists: Luke's four blessings contained in Matthew's eight, both placing poverty first as the purest virtue. The blessing is applied directly to the disciples who had left all (Henry): "yours is the kingdom of God." The verse opens the Sermon with the great reversal of the gospel: the poor, despised by the world, are blessed and heirs of the kingdom. Christ lifts his eyes on his disciples to reveal the hidden light of his word, and pronounces blessed those whom the world counts wretched — the poor who, for his sake, are rich in faith and heirs of heaven.
21:
"Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh." The witnesses note the second and third Beatitudes, again in Luke's direct form. The eastern Cyril of Alexandria notes the same pattern as v.20: where Matthew has "hunger and thirst after righteousness," Luke has simply "hunger," the Evangelists "dividing the narrative." Gregory of Nyssa reads the spiritual sense: "to those who hunger and thirst after righteousness He promises abundance... none of the pleasures of this life can satisfy, but the pursuit of virtue alone is followed by that reward which implants a joy in the soul that never fails."

On weeping and laughing, Chrysostom: "godly sorrow is a great thing, and it works repentance to salvation... Such grief is the source of gladness... We often find the Lord sorrowing, never laughing." Basil guards the "laugh": "not the noise of laughter from the mouth, but a gladness pure and unmixed with aught of sorrow."

The grammatical voices note Luke's sharpening "now." Robertson: "Luke adds this adverb here and in the next sentence after weep. This sharpens the contrast between present sufferings and the future blessings." Ellicott notes the "laugh" is "the only instance in the New Testament of the use of laughter as the symbol of spiritual joy," the Aramaic word our Lord used having "a somewhat higher meaning" than the Greek.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ blesses those who hunger and weep now — again in Luke's direct form (without Matthew's "after righteousness") — promising they shall be filled and shall laugh. The fathers read both the literal and spiritual sense: those who hunger after righteousness shall be satisfied with the joy of virtue (Gregory of Nyssa); godly sorrow, the source of gladness, shall give place to a pure, unmixed joy (Chrysostom, Basil). Luke's sharpening "now" sets the present suffering against the future blessing (Robertson): the hunger and tears of the present are the seed of the fullness and laughter to come. The verse continues the great reversal of the gospel: those who suffer want and sorrow for Christ's sake — and after a godly sort — are blessed, for their present grief is the preparatory of future joy. The kingdom turns the world's values upside down: the hungry filled, the weeping made to laugh, in the day of God's reward.
22:
"Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man's sake." The witnesses note the Beatitude on persecution and its distinctively Lukan elements. Bede (under v.20) read the escalation: "Let them separate and expel you from the synagogue, Christ finds you out... Let them reproach the name of the Crucified, He raises them with Him... Here he means the name of Christian, which by Jews and Gentiles was frequently erased and cast out."

The grammatical voices explain the Lukan clauses. Ellicott: "The clause when they shall separate you from their company is peculiar to St. Luke, and refers to the excommunication or exclusion from the synagogue (John 16:2)." Clarke: "They will excommunicate you... and cast out their name likewise, as a thing evil in itself. By your name is meant their name as his disciples" — Nazarenes or Christians, "matter of reproach in the mouths of their enemies." Robertson notes the name "Christian or disciple or Nazarene came to be a byword of contempt as shown in the Acts," and the persecution under Nero "when Christianity was not a religio licita."

The phrase "for the Son of man's sake" the witnesses note as the qualifying ground of the blessing. The hatred is blessed not when deserved for crime but when borne for Christ. Robertson: "Jesus foretold what will befall those who are loyal to him. The Acts of the Apostles is a commentary on this prophecy."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ blesses those hated, excommunicated, reproached, and slandered "for the Son of man's sake" — the qualifying ground, for the hatred is blessed only when borne for Christ, not deserved for crime. The Lukan clause "separate you from their company" points to exclusion from the synagogue (John 16:2, Ellicott); "cast out your name as evil" to the contempt heaped on the name Christian (Clarke, Robertson), fulfilled in the Acts and the Neronian persecution. The verse blesses the persecuted disciple: the world's hatred, exclusion, and slander, borne for Christ, are no curse but a blessing, for they mark the faithful follower of the Son of man. The fathers note that what men cast out, Christ takes up and raises with himself. The Beatitude prepares the disciples for the cost of discipleship — and turns the world's worst treatment into the badge of blessing, for the persecuted share the lot of the prophets (v.23) and the reward of heaven.
23:
"Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets." The witnesses note the command to rejoice in persecution and the prophets' precedent. Gill: "leap for joy; as if the greatest honour and happiness imaginable had been conferred on them." The grammatical voices note Luke's vivid word. Ellicott: "Leap for joy. The word is peculiar to St. Luke in the New Testament, and occurs elsewhere only in Luke 1:41, 44." Robertson: "It answers to Matthew's be exceeding glad."

On "your reward is great in heaven," the fathers (under v.20) measured the greatness by the speaker. Chrysostom: "Great and little are measured by the dignity of the speaker... now it is the Lord in whose hands are eternal treasures and riches surpassing man's conception, who has promised great reward." Basil: the reward is great "not as in comparison with those things in our power, but as being in itself great because given by God."

The prophets' precedent the witnesses read as both comfort and pattern. Eusebius (under v.20): "he fortifies His disciples against the attacks of their adversaries... For in like manner did their fathers to the prophets." The disciples who suffer for Christ stand in the noble succession of the persecuted prophets, and shall receive a prophet's reward.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ commands the persecuted to rejoice and leap for joy (Luke's vivid word, used also of the unborn John, 1:41), for their reward is great in heaven — great because promised by the Lord in whose hands are eternal treasures (Chrysostom), great in itself because given by God (Basil). The precedent of the prophets, whom their fathers persecuted likewise, is both comfort and pattern: the suffering disciples stand in the noble succession of the persecuted prophets and shall share their reward. The verse crowns the persecution Beatitude with the command to exult: the world's hatred, borne for Christ, is cause not for sorrow but for leaping joy, for it marks the faithful as heirs of a great heavenly reward and members of the prophetic line. The disciple persecuted for the Son of man's sake may rejoice, knowing his suffering is the badge of the prophets and the pledge of heaven's recompense.
24:
"But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation." The witnesses note the first of the Woes — the dark counterpart to the Beatitudes, found only in Luke. Cyril: "Having said before that poverty for God's sake is the cause of every good thing... he goes on to denounce the opposite as the source of condemnation." Ambrose guards the woe: "it is not those who possess riches, but those who know not how to use them, that are condemned... It is not therefore the money, but the heart of the possessor which is in fault."

The Reformed Calvin treats the structure and the qualification. He notes Luke "contrasts four woes with the four blessings, so that the clauses mutually correspond." On the rich: "He pronounces a curse on the rich — not on all the rich, but on those who receive their consolation in the world; that is, who are so completely occupied with their worldly possessions that they forget the life to come... riches are so far from making a man happy, that they often become the means of his destruction. In any other point of view, the rich are not excluded from the kingdom" — as poor Lazarus was received into rich Abraham's bosom (Augustine).

On "ye have received your consolation," the grammatical voices note the force. Ellicott: "ye have your consolation — i.e., all that you understand or care for, all that you can have." Robertson: "Receipt in full apecho means as the papyri show." The rich who fix their hope on earth have already drawn out their full reward; nothing remains for the world to come.

The witnesses agree: the first Woe falls on the rich — but, the witnesses carefully qualify, not on all the rich, but on those who fix their hope on earth and forget the life to come (Calvin), whose heart, not their money, is at fault (Ambrose). "Ye have received your consolation" means they have drawn their full reward already (Robertson, Ellicott); nothing remains. The Woes are the dark counterpart of the Beatitudes, peculiar to Luke, balancing blessing with warning. The verse warns against the peril of wealth that lulls the soul: the rich who find their whole comfort in this world have spent it, and face want in the next (as the parable of Dives and Lazarus, Luke 16, illustrates). It is not riches but trust in riches that condemns; and the warning calls the prosperous to hold their wealth loosely and seek the consolation that endures.
25:
"Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep." The witnesses note the second and third Woes, balancing the second and third Beatitudes. Bede: "That rich man clothed in purple was full, feasting sumptuously every day, but endured in hunger that dreadful woe, when from the finger of Lazarus he begged a drop of water." Basil reads the woe on laughter: "Whereas the Lord reproves those who laugh now, it is plain that there will never be a house of laughter to the faithful, especially since there is so great a multitude of those who die in sin for whom we must mourn. Excessive laughter is a sign of want of moderation."

The grammatical voices note the reversal. Robertson: "Here twice as in verse 21 in contrast with future punishment. The joys and sorrows in these two verses are turned round, measure for measure reversed. The Rich Man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31) illustrate these contrasts." Ellicott connects "that laugh now" to James 4:9: "Let your laughter be turned to mourning."

The Reformed Calvin reads "the full" and "those who laugh" of the carnally satisfied: those "lifted up by confidence in the blessings of the present life," and "who have given themselves up to Epicurean mirth, who are plunged in carnal pleasures, and spurn every kind of trouble." Gill reads them also of the self-satisfied in their own righteousness, who "do not hunger and thirst after Christ."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the second and third Woes fall on the full and the laughing — the carnally satisfied and the careless mirthful — who shall hunger, mourn, and weep. The reversal is exact (Robertson): the joys and sorrows of vv.21 and 25 are turned round, measure for measure, as in the parable of Dives and Lazarus (Luke 16). The full who feast now (like the rich man) shall hunger; those who laugh in carnal mirth (Calvin) shall mourn. The verse warns against the soul lulled by present satisfaction and frivolous mirth, forgetting the multitude who die in sin and the judgment to come (Basil). The Woes balance the Beatitudes with solemn warning: the present fullness and laughter of the worldly are no blessing but a peril, for they shall be reversed in the day of God. The gospel's great reversal cuts both ways — the hungry filled, but the full emptied; the weeping made to laugh, but the laughing made to weep.
26:
"Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets." The witnesses note the fourth and final Woe — on universal popularity — balancing the persecution Beatitude. Gill notes the word "all": "it looks ill in persons, when the men of the world, wicked men, all of them, applaud and commend them; for this can never be, if they are truly religious... it is no bad sign, to have the good word of good men, and therefore these must be excepted."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as a warning against ambition, especially for teachers: "nothing is more common than to seek the applauses of men... he points out to them that the favour of men would prove to be their ruin. This warning refers peculiarly to teachers, who have no plague more to be dreaded than ambition: because it is impossible for them not to corrupt the pure doctrine of God, when they seek to please men (Galatians 1:10)." He clarifies: it is "the wicked favour of the flesh that is here condemned: for faithful ministers of sound doctrine enjoy the applause of good men."

The grammatical voices note the precedent and its lesson. Ellicott: the false prophets, praised by all, "open a wide question as to the worth of praise as a test of human conduct, and tend to a conclusion quite the reverse of the maxim, Vox populi, vox Dei. Truth... is often, if not always, on the side of the minority, sometimes even on that of one who is as an Athanasius contra mundum." Yet he balances: praise and good repute have their value as the witness of the moral sense (1 Timothy 3:7).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the Woes. The witnesses agree: the fourth Woe falls on those whom all men praise — for so the false prophets were praised, while the true prophets were persecuted (v.23). Universal popularity, especially when it includes the applause of the wicked, is an ill sign of unfaithfulness; the faithful, who reprove sin, cannot please all (Gill). Calvin warns especially teachers against the ambition that corrupts doctrine to please men (Galatians 1:10), while noting that the favour of good men is no curse. Ellicott draws the lesson that truth often lies with the persecuted minority, against "Vox populi, vox Dei." The verse balances the persecution Beatitude: as the world's hatred (borne for Christ) is a blessing, so its universal praise (the lot of the false prophets) is a warning. The faithful disciple need not court the world's applause, but should fear it when it comes from all — for the false prophets, not the true, were the ones whom all men praised.
27:
"But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you." The witnesses note the great command of enemy-love, the heart of the Sermon. The fathers exalt it. Ambrose: "among the three greatest (hope, faith, and charity), the greatest is charity, which is commanded in these words, Love your enemies." Chrysostom: "He says not, Do not hate, but love; nor did He merely command to love, but also to do good." He likens the believer to a physician toward the mad: "physicians, when they are attacked by madmen, have then most compassion on them... Have you also a like consideration towards your persecutors; for it is they who are under the greatest infirmity."

The grammatical voices note "which hear." Gill takes it of those who had heard the Pharisees' traditions, to which Christ's commands are opposed; Henry takes it broadly: "to all you that hear... for these are lessons of universal concern." The command, opposed to the traditional "hate thine enemy," extends love beyond the natural limit.

The fathers read enemy-love as the perfection of virtue and the imitation of God. Chrysostom: "He has ordained nothing surpassing our nature, but what He had long before implanted in our conscience." Cyril notes it was "well adapted to the holy teachers who were about to preach throughout the earth," for vengeance would have hindered their calling of persecutors to salvation.

The thin comment matches the rich verse, which opens the section on enemy-love. The witnesses agree: Christ commands the highest of virtues — to love enemies and do good to those who hate — the heart of the Sermon and the perfection of charity (Ambrose). It is not enough not to hate; we must actively love and do good (Chrysostom), regarding our persecutors as the physician regards the mad, with compassion for their infirmity. The command, addressed to all who hear (Henry) and opposed to the traditional "hate thine enemy," extends love beyond every natural limit, making the believer like God himself (v.35). The verse displays the distinctive ethic of Christ's kingdom: not the reciprocal love of the natural man (v.32), but a love that embraces even enemies, blesses cursers, and prays for persecutors. This love, the perfection of virtue and the imitation of the Father, was especially fitting for the apostles who would call their persecutors to salvation. Enemy-love is the badge of the children of the Most High.
28:
"Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you." The brief verse continues the command of enemy-love into blessing and prayer. Gill notes the simple sense: to bless those who curse and pray for those who mistreat. Chrysostom (under v.27) had drawn out the wrongness of cursing: "they who pierce their own souls deserve tears and weeping, not curses... Your mouth was given you not to bite with, but to heal the wounds of others. But he commands us to count our enemies in the rank of our friends... Pray for them which persecute you."

Chrysostom (under v.27) also rebuked those who pray against their enemies: "many on the contrary, stretching forth their hands, pray God not for their sins, but against their enemies, which is nothing else but piercing their own selves... It is a time of forgiveness, prayer, and mourning, not of rage." To pray against enemies in God's presence is to defeat one's own prayer.

The command to bless and pray the witnesses read as the positive expression of enemy-love. It is not enough to refrain from cursing; the disciple must actively bless and intercede for those who wrong him — returning good for evil in word (blessing) and in prayer (intercession). This, the witnesses note, is the imitation of Christ, who prayed for his crucifiers.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the command of enemy-love extends to blessing those who curse and praying for those who mistreat — the positive expression of returning good for evil. Chrysostom rebukes the cursing tongue (given to heal, not to bite) and those who pray against their enemies (defeating their own prayer in a time meant for forgiveness). The disciple must not merely refrain from retaliation but actively bless and intercede for those who wrong him, in imitation of Christ, who prayed for his crucifiers ("Father, forgive them"). The verse displays the depth of the kingdom's ethic: enemy-love is not passive endurance but active good — blessing on the lips and intercession in prayer for the very persecutors. To bless the curser and pray for the abuser is to be a child of the Most High (v.35) and to share the heart of the Lord who blessed and prayed for those who nailed him to the cross. This is the love that conquers evil with good.
29:
"And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloke forbid not to take thy coat also." The witnesses note the command of non-retaliation. The puritan Henry expounds it: "We must not be rigorous in revenging a wrong... Unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek, instead of bringing an action against him, offer also the other; that is, pass it by... leave it to God to plead thy cause, and do thou sit down silent under the affront. When we do thus, God will smite our enemies... for he hath said, Vengeance is mine." On the cloak: "Let him have that too, rather than fight for it... If a man run away in thy debt, and take away thy goods with him, do not perplex thyself."

The fathers (under v.27) read the patient endurance as overcoming evil. Cyril: "the Lord would have us to be despisers of property... For this is the soul's virtue, which is altogether alien from the pleasure of wealth." Chrysostom: "Go on wisely, and prepare yourself to suffer what he desires you to do; overcoming his insolence by your great prudence, that he may depart with shame at your excellent endurance."

The command the witnesses understand not as forbidding all self-defence or justice, but as forbidding the vindictive, retaliatory spirit. The disciple is to yield his rights rather than fight for them in a spirit of revenge, leaving vengeance to God and overcoming evil by patient endurance.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ commands non-retaliation — to offer the other cheek and yield the coat as well as the cloak — forbidding not all justice but the vindictive, retaliatory spirit. The disciple is to suffer wrong patiently rather than avenge it (Henry), leaving vengeance to God who has said "Vengeance is mine," and despising property for the soul's sake (Cyril). The patient endurance overcomes the wrongdoer's insolence and shames him by its meekness (Chrysostom). The verse displays the kingdom's ethic of yielding rather than retaliating: the disciple, like his Master who "when he was reviled, reviled not again," bears injury without vengeance, conquering evil by patient good. It is not a charter for injustice or a forbidding of all defence, but a call to renounce the spirit of revenge and to overcome evil with the patient endurance that leaves all to God. The cheek offered and the coat yielded are the marks of a heart freed from vindictiveness and trusting in God to plead its cause.
30:
"Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again." The witnesses note the command of free giving and non-demanding. The puritan Henry: "We must be free in giving to them that need... Give to every one that is a proper object of charity, that wants necessaries, which thou hast wherewithal to supply out of thy superfluities... Christ would have his disciples ready to distribute, and willing to communicate, to their power in ordinary cases, and beyond their power in extraordinary."

The fathers (under v.27) qualify the giving with wisdom. Augustine: "He says not, To him that seeks give all things, but give what you justly and honestly can, that is, what neither hurts you nor another: and if you have justly refused any one, the justice must be declared to him... sometimes you will confer a greater boon when you have corrected him who seeks what he ought not." Chrysostom warns against using a beggar's idleness as an excuse for hardness: "If we spend upon all indifferently, we shall always have compassion: for because Abraham entertains all, he also entertains angels."

On "ask them not again," Chrysostom (under v.27) reflects on stewardship: "Everything we have we receive from God. But when we speak of mine and thine, they are only bare words... God wishes those things to be yours which are entrusted to you for your brethren." The disciple is to give freely and not anxiously reclaim what is taken, holding earthly goods loosely as a steward.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ commands free, generous giving to all who need (out of one's superfluities ordinarily, beyond one's power extraordinarily, Henry), and a non-demanding spirit toward those who take one's goods. The fathers qualify the giving with wisdom: give what is just and honest, sometimes correcting the unworthy seeker (Augustine), yet without using idleness as an excuse for hardness (Chrysostom). The command rests on stewardship: all we have is God's, entrusted to us for our brethren (Chrysostom), so we hold it loosely and give freely. The verse displays the kingdom's ethic of open-handed generosity: the disciple, freed from grasping and anxious self-protection, gives to the needy and does not anxiously reclaim what is taken. This liberality, tempered by wisdom, flows from the recognition that we are stewards, not owners — and that the God who gives all to us would have us give freely to others. The open hand is the mark of the child of the generous Father.
31:
"And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise." The witnesses note the golden rule, the summary of the law of love. The puritan Henry: "We must render to all their due, and be honest and just in all our dealings... What we should expect, in reason, to be done to us, either in justice or charity, by others, if they were in our condition and we in theirs, that we must do to them. We must put our souls into their souls' stead, and then pity and succour them, as we should desire and justly expect to be ourselves pitied and succoured."

Chrysostom (under v.27) noted the natural law in it: "He does not say, Whatever you would not that men should do to you, do not you. For since there are two ways which lead to virtue, namely abstaining from evil and doing good, he names one, signifying the other also." The positive form — do good as you would be done by — surpasses the merely negative golden rule of the rabbis.

The grammatical voices note its placement. Robertson and others observe Luke sets the golden rule in the midst of the enemy-love section, where it grounds the call to love beyond reciprocity: do to others as you would be done by, not as they have done to you. The rule, Chrysostom noted, appeals to the natural law written in the conscience: "your own will is the law to you."

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the golden rule — do to others as you would have them do to you — is the summary of the law of love, grounded in putting one's soul in the other's place (Henry). Its positive form (do good, not merely abstain from evil) surpasses the negative golden rule of the rabbis (Chrysostom) and names one way to virtue while signifying both. The rule appeals to the natural law written in the conscience: our own will, rightly applied, is the law to us. The verse, set in the midst of the enemy-love section, grounds the call to love beyond reciprocity: the disciple is to treat others not as they have treated him but as he would wish to be treated. This is the heart of practical love — the imaginative transfer that pities and succours others as we would wish to be pitied and succoured. The golden rule distils the whole law of love into a single, searching standard, by which every dealing with our neighbour, friend or enemy, is to be measured.
32:
"For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them." The witnesses note Christ's contrast between natural reciprocal love and the higher love he commands. Chrysostom: "There are several causes which produce love; but spiritual love exceeds them all. For nothing earthly engenders it... but it descends from heaven... It is common to all to do good to those who do good to them. But he shows that he seeks something more than is the custom of sinners." He instances Paul going to those who stoned him, and Moses praying for those who stoned him.

The grammatical voices note the term "sinners." Clarke: "I believe the word is used by St. Luke in the same sense in which tax-gatherers is used by St. Matthew, and signifies heathens." Ellicott: Luke, "writing for Gentiles," uses "the wider term sinners, instead of the more specific publicans" of Matthew. The contrast is the same: even the irreligious love those who love them; the disciple must do more.

On "what thank have ye?", Robertson: "What grace or gratitude is there to you? Matthew has reward (misthon)." Wesley draws the lesson: "our Lord has so little regard for one of the highest instances of natural virtue, namely, the returning love for love, that he does not account it even to deserve thanks. For even sinners do the same... Therefore he may do this, who has not taken one step in Christianity."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ contrasts the natural, reciprocal love of sinners (loving those who love them) with the higher, heavenly love he commands. Reciprocal love deserves no "thank" or reward, for even the irreligious — heathens, "sinners" (Luke's wider term for Matthew's "publicans," Ellicott) — practise it. Spiritual love, by contrast, descends from heaven and embraces even enemies (Chrysostom), as Paul and Moses loved their persecutors. Wesley draws the sharp lesson: returning love for love, though a high natural virtue, is no mark of Christianity, for even those who do not know God do it. The verse exposes the inadequacy of merely natural love and presses the disciple toward the distinctive love of the kingdom — a love that, like God's, reaches beyond those who return it to the unthankful and the enemy (v.35). To love only our friends is to do no more than sinners; the child of God is called to a love that descends from heaven and surpasses nature.
33:
"And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same." The brief verse continues the contrast into doing good. Gill: "As one good turn deserves another" — even this reciprocal kindness is no more than sinners do. Robertson: "Even the sinners, the article distinguishing the class. Matthew has even the publicans and even the Gentiles. That completes the list of the outcasts for sinners includes harlots and all the rest."

Ellicott (under v.32) noted Luke's substitution of "actual deeds of kindness" for the "salutations" of Matthew's parallel — Luke making the point concrete in doing good rather than mere greeting. The principle is the same: reciprocal good-doing, like reciprocal love, is the common practice of even the irreligious and deserves no special reward.

The repetition the witnesses read as Christ's pressing of the point. Three times (love, doing good, lending) he shows that the merely reciprocal — love for love, good for good, loan for loan — is no more than sinners do, and so no mark of the kingdom. The disciple is called beyond this natural reciprocity to a love that does good without expectation of return (v.35).

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: Christ presses the contrast — to do good to those who do good to you is no more than sinners do, and deserves no "thank." Luke makes the point concrete in deeds of kindness (where Matthew has salutations, Ellicott), and uses "sinners" for the whole class of outcasts (Robertson). The threefold repetition (love, good-doing, lending) drives home that merely reciprocal virtue — good for good — is the common practice of the irreligious and no mark of the kingdom. The verse continues to expose the inadequacy of natural reciprocity: the disciple is called beyond returning good for good to a love that does good freely, without expectation of return, even to enemies and the unthankful (v.35).
