To do good only to those who do good to us is to remain on the level of sinners; the child of the Most High is summoned to a higher good-doing that mirrors the free, unrewarded goodness of God himself, who is kind to the unthankful and the evil.
34:
"And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again." The witnesses note the third instance of merely reciprocal virtue — lending in expectation of return. The grammatical voices explain the verb. Robertson: "danizo, to lend for interest in a business transaction... To receive again as much (that they may get back the equal — principal and interest, apparently)." Ellicott notes the Jewish setting: "receiving interest on the loan is not contemplated at all" among Jewish hearers.

The early latin-father Tertullian connects it to the law's preparation for the gospel: "compare the words of Ezekiel, He hath not given his money upon usury, nor will he take any increase... this was the function of the law as preparatory to the gospel... inculcating a benevolence which as yet expressed itself but falteringly." The law forbade usury; the gospel goes further, to lending without hope of return.

The instance the witnesses read as completing the threefold contrast. To lend expecting repayment (with or without interest) is what sinners do; the disciple is called to lend freely, "hoping for nothing again" (v.35). The natural calculation of return — lending to get back as much — is no mark of the kingdom's love.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the third instance of merely reciprocal virtue is lending to those from whom one hopes to receive — even with interest (Robertson), though interest is not contemplated among the Jewish hearers (Ellicott). Like reciprocal love and good-doing, this is what sinners do, deserving no "thank." Tertullian sets it in the law's progress toward the gospel: the law forbade usury, the gospel goes further to lending without hope of return. The verse completes the threefold contrast (love, good, lending), each showing that natural reciprocity — calculating a return — is no mark of the kingdom's love. The disciple is called beyond the prudent lending that expects repayment to a free liberality that gives without calculating gain (v.35). To lend only where return is sure is to remain on the level of sinners; the child of God lends freely, looking not to earthly repayment but to the great reward of heaven and the imitation of the Father who gives to the unthankful.
35:
"But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil." The witnesses note the climactic command and a famous translation crux. On "love your enemies," Clarke: "the most sublime precept ever delivered to man: a false religion durst not give a precept of this nature, because, without supernatural influence, it must be for ever impracticable."

A genuine crux concerns "hoping for nothing again." The grammatical voices favour "never despairing." Ellicott: the verb's "uniform signification in the LXX is that of giving up hope, despairing... Give and lend according to the law of Christ, and do not let the absence of immediate profit make you lose heart." Robertson: "the correct reading there is elpizein, not apelpizein... the word occurs in Polybius, Diodorus, LXX with the sense of despairing." He records the grave consequence of the Vulgate's misreading "nihil desperantes" (hoping for nothing thence): "On the strength of it Popes and councils have repeatedly condemned the taking of any interest whatever for loans... money lending passed into the hands of the Jews, and added greatly to the unnatural detestation in which Jews were held" (Plummer).

The Reformed Calvin insists the verse is not about usury at all: "It is a mistake to confine this statement to usury... Christ only exhorts us to perform our duties freely, and tells us that mercenary acts are of no account in the sight of God... When believers lend, they ought to go beyond heathens; they ought to exercise pure liberality." On "children of the Highest," Robertson notes all real children of God are so termed, and "God the Father is kind towards the unkind and wicked."

The witnesses agree on the substance — the disciple is to love enemies, do good, and lend freely, in imitation of God who is kind to the unthankful and evil, that he may be a child of the Most High and receive a great reward — while dividing on the text of "hoping for nothing again": the Authorized reading, or (the grammatical preference) "never despairing" (not losing heart at the absence of return). Calvin insists the verse concerns not usury but disinterested liberality. The verse is the climax of the enemy-love section: the disciple, loving and giving freely without calculating return, mirrors the free goodness of the Father who blesses even his enemies, and so proves himself a true child of God. The great reward is God himself; and the imitation of the kind, generous Father — not the reciprocal virtue of sinners — is the badge of the children of the Most High.
36:
"Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful." The witnesses note the summary command of mercy, Luke's form of Matthew's "be perfect." Ellicott: "merciful takes the place of perfect, as being the noblest of the divine attributes, in which all others reach their completeness." Robertson: "In both the perfection of the Father is placed as the goal before his children. In neither case is it said that they have reached it."

Clarke expounds the word: "compassionate... As God is ever disposed to give all necessary help and support to those who are miserable, so his followers, being influenced by the same spirit, are easy to be entreated, and are at all times ready to contribute to the uttermost of their power to relieve the miseries of the distressed. A merciful man easily forgets injuries; pardons them without being solicited; and does not permit repeated returns of ingratitude to deter him from doing good, even to the unthankful and the unholy."

The command the witnesses read as the summary and ground of all the preceding — enemy-love, free giving, non-retaliation all flow from mercy, the imitation of the merciful Father. Cyril (under v.32): "Great is the praise of mercy. For this virtue makes us like to God, and imprints upon our souls certain signs of a heavenly nature." To be merciful as the Father is merciful is to bear the family likeness of the children of God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which summarizes the section. The witnesses agree: Christ commands mercy as the summary of all — "be merciful as your Father is merciful," Luke's form of Matthew's "be perfect," for mercy is the noblest divine attribute in which all others are completed (Ellicott). The merciful man, like God, readily helps the miserable, forgets injuries, pardons unsolicited, and does good even to the unthankful (Clarke). Mercy is the ground of all the preceding commands — enemy-love, free giving, non-retaliation all flow from it — and the family likeness of the children of God: this virtue makes us like to God and stamps the heavenly nature on the soul (Cyril). The verse sets the imitation of the merciful Father as the goal of the disciple, not yet attained but ever pursued. To be merciful as God is merciful is the heart of the kingdom's ethic and the mark of the true child of the Most High — for the children must bear the likeness of their Father, whose mercy is over all his works.
37:
"Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven." The witnesses note the command against censorious judgment. The fathers qualify it. Ambrose: "we must not readily judge others, lest when conscious of guilt yourself, you should be compelled to pass sentence upon another." Chrysostom: "Judge not your superior... nor a sinner the innocent. You must not blame them, but advise and correct with love; neither must we pass judgment in doubtful and indifferent matters." Gregory of Nyssa: "he does not forbid judgment with pardon." Bede: "he bids us forgive injuries, and show kindness, and our sins shall be forgiven us."

The grammatical voices note the threefold escalation — judge, condemn, forgive — and the corresponding promise. The censorious, harsh judgment of others is forbidden, not all moral discernment; and the promise is reciprocal: as we judge, condemn, or forgive, so shall we be dealt with. Cyril (under the passage): "as the merciful and meek man dispels the rage of sinners, so the harsh and cruel adds to his own crimes."

The command the witnesses read as flowing from the mercy of v.36. The merciful Father's children must not be harsh judges of others but forgiving, leaving judgment to God. The forbidding is of the censorious, fault-finding spirit (the mote-and-beam of vv.41-42), not of all just discernment between good and evil.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ forbids the censorious, harsh judgment of others — not all moral discernment, but the fault-finding spirit that condemns while guilty itself (Ambrose), judges superiors and the innocent (Chrysostom), and judges doubtful matters. He commands instead to forgive, with the reciprocal promise: as we judge, condemn, or forgive, so shall we be dealt with by God. The fathers carefully qualify: judgment with pardon is not forbidden (Gregory of Nyssa), nor the loving correction of sin, but the rash, censorious condemnation. The command flows from the mercy of v.36: the children of the merciful Father must be forgiving, not harsh, leaving final judgment to God. The verse displays the kingdom's ethic of forbearance: the disciple, conscious of his own faults, is to forgive rather than condemn, and so to receive forgiveness. To judge harshly is to invite harsh judgment; to forgive is to be forgiven — the measure we mete being measured back to us (v.38).
38:
"Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again." The witnesses note the promise of overflowing recompense for generosity. The grammatical voices unfold the imagery. Lightfoot supplies rabbinic background on the heaped and stricken measures of the Temple. Barnes: "Good measure — pressed down, as figs or grapes might be... shaken together, to make it more compact... running over, so full that the measure would overflow." On "into your bosom," Robertson: "The fold of the wide upper garment bound by the girdle made a pocket in common use" (Exodus 4:6; Proverbs 6:27).

On the recompense, Clarke: "Christian charity will make no difficulty in giving that which eternal truth promises to restore... there is none but God who receives no advantage from his gifts, and who engages to pay these debts of his creatures with an excessive interest." Wesley marvels: "Amazing goodness! So we are permitted even to carve for ourselves! We ourselves are to tell God how much mercy he shall show us... Give then to man, what thou designest to receive of God."

On "with the same measure," the fathers (under v.37) note the principle of reciprocity. Theophylact resolves the apparent tension between "good measure running over" and "the same measure": the merciful receive mercy a thousandfold, yet it is still "the same measure" in kind. Basil: "according to the same measure with which each one of you metes, in doing good works or sinning, will he receive reward or punishment."

The witnesses agree: Christ promises that generous giving is met with overflowing recompense — good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, given into the bosom (the garment's fold). The God who alone receives no advantage from his gifts repays his creatures' charity with "excessive interest" (Clarke); and the measure we mete is measured back to us — so that, amazingly, we determine our own recompense by our generosity (Wesley). The fathers resolve the tension: the reciprocity is in kind ("the same measure"), but the divine return is overflowing ("good measure running over"). The verse displays the abundant generosity of God toward the generous: liberality to others is repaid by God a thousandfold, in this life or the next. The principle works both ways — mercy for the merciful, judgment for the harsh (v.37) — so that the disciple is moved to open-handed giving, knowing that what he metes to others God will measure back to him, pressed down and running over.
39:
"And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch?" The witnesses note the parable of the blind guide. Cyril reads it of the teachers: "His disciples were the future teachers of the world... that they should not be blind leaders of the blind." Theophylact applies it to the censorious: "If you judge another, and in the very same way sin yourself, are not you like to the blind leading the blind? For how can you lead him to good when you also yourself commit sin?" Bede connects it to the preceding: "If anger has blinded you against the violent, and avarice against the grasping, how can you with your corrupt heart cure his corruption?"

The grammatical voices note the structure. Robertson: "Plummer thinks that the second half of the sermon begins here as indicated by Luke's insertion of And he spake." He notes the wider sense of "parable" for "crisp proverbs." Calvin observes Luke gives the saying "without mentioning any occurrence," as he records many of Christ's discourses; it may have been spoken more than once.

The application the witnesses read as a warning to teachers and the censorious. One who is himself blind (in sin or error) cannot safely guide another; both will fall. The hypocrite who would correct his brother's mote while blind to his own beam (vv.41-42) is the blind leading the blind. Self-knowledge and self-correction must precede the guidance of others.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's parable of the blind guide warns that one who is himself blind — in sin, error, or censorious hypocrisy — cannot safely lead another; both will fall into the ditch. The application is double: to teachers, who must have their own minds illuminated lest they be blind leaders (Cyril); and to the censorious, who judge others while sinning themselves (Theophylact, Bede). The verse, opening the latter half of the Sermon (Robertson), connects to the mote-and-beam (vv.41-42): the hypocrite blind to his own fault cannot remove his brother's. Self-knowledge and self-correction must precede the guidance or correction of others. The parable displays the folly and danger of presuming to lead or judge while oneself blind — a warning especially to those who would teach or correct. The blind cannot guide the blind; only one whose own eye is cleared (v.42) can help his brother see. The would-be guide must first be guided himself.
40:
"The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master." The witnesses note the proverb of the disciple and master and debate its application here. Barnes reads it of the blind guide: "The learner is not above his teacher... if they were blind, their followers would be also; therefore it was important for them to understand fully the doctrines of the gospel, and not to be blind leaders of the blind." Ellicott connects it to the judging theme: the disciples were to follow their Master, who "taught, but did not judge (John 8:11-15)... Were they above their Master that they should do what He had refrained from doing?"

On "every one that is perfect," the grammatical voices clarify the word. Clarke: "thoroughly instructed... from katartizo, to adjust, adapt, knit together, restore. Every one who is thoroughly instructed in Divine things... though he cannot be above, yet will be as his teacher — holy, harmless, undefiled." Robertson: "the state of completion, perfect passive participle... it is a long process to get the pupil patched up to the plane of his teacher." Calvin and Ellicott prefer the sense of resemblance: the perfected disciple becomes like his master, not above him.

The connection of thought the witnesses admit is somewhat obscure. Ellicott: "we may not unreasonably believe that some links have been omitted." But the application is clear: the disciple, formed by his master, will resemble him — for good (if the master is Christ) or for ill (if blind guides). The disciples are to model themselves on Christ, their true Master, and so abstain from the censorious judgment he himself refrained from.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the proverb "the disciple is not above his master" teaches that the disciple, when fully instructed, becomes like his master — for the blind guide produces blind followers (Barnes), and the disciple of Christ must model himself on the Master who taught but did not judge (Ellicott). "Perfect" (better, "perfected") means thoroughly instructed and formed into resemblance (Clarke, Calvin), not above but like the teacher. The verse, though its connection is somewhat obscure (Ellicott suggests omitted links), applies the principle of imitation: the disciple becomes like his master, so the choice of master is decisive. Those led by blind guides become blind; those formed by Christ become like Christ — holy, harmless, and abstaining from the harsh judgment he refrained from. The verse calls the disciple to be shaped into the likeness of his true Master, and warns that he will rise no higher than the one he follows. To follow Christ is to be perfected into his image.
41:
"And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?" The witnesses note the parable of the mote and beam — the climax of the warning against censoriousness. Gill: "a lesser sin in comparison of others... though none are to be countenanced, yet some are not so severely to be animadverted upon as others." Cyril (under v.39): "How can he who is guilty of grievous sins (the beam) condemn him who has sinned only slightly (the mote)?" Theophylact: the words apply "especially to teachers, who while they punish the least sins of those under them, leave their own unpunished."

The grammatical voices note the vivid contrast. Robertson: "Mote (karphos) and beam (dokon)." The mote is a tiny speck, the beam a great rafter — the disproportion between the small fault we condemn in others and the great one we ignore in ourselves. The contrast is deliberately exaggerated to expose the absurdity of censorious hypocrisy.

The parable the witnesses read as the heart of "judge not" (v.37). The censorious man scrutinizes his brother's small fault while blind to his own great one — the blind leading the blind (v.39). The remedy (v.42) is self-examination first: cast out your own beam, then you will see clearly to help your brother.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the mote and beam expose the absurdity of censorious hypocrisy — scrutinizing the brother's small fault (the speck) while blind to one's own great sin (the rafter). The disproportion is deliberately exaggerated (Robertson) to shame the fault-finder. The parable applies especially to teachers who punish small sins in others while ignoring their own (Theophylact), and is the heart of "judge not" (v.37): the censorious man, blind to his own beam, is the blind leading the blind (v.39). The verse displays the self-deception of the harsh judge, quick to see others' faults and slow to see his own. The remedy follows (v.42): self-examination and self-correction first, then the clear sight to help one's brother. To behold the mote while ignoring the beam is the mark of the hypocrite; the disciple is called first to remove his own great fault before presuming to correct his brother's small one. Self-judgment must precede the judgment of others.
42:
"Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother's eye." The witnesses note the exposure of the hypocrite and the remedy. The grammatical voices mark the contrast. Robertson: "Thou hypocrite — contrast to the studied politeness of brother above. Powerful picture of blind self-complacence and incompetence, the keyword to the argument here." The censor addresses his brother with feigned care while blind to his own greater fault.

Theophylact (under v.39): "the Lord calls them hypocrites, because to this end judge they the sins of others, that they themselves might seem just." The hypocrisy lies in the pretence of helping a brother while harbouring a greater sin — making the censure of others a cloak for one's own unrighteousness.

The remedy the witnesses note is not to abandon all correction but to reverse the order: "cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly." Self-correction qualifies one to help others. Augustine (under v.6 area) and the fathers: first be cleansed of great sins, then give counsel to a brother guilty of slight ones. The correction of others is not forbidden but made dependent on self-examination first.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ exposes the hypocrite who offers to remove his brother's mote while blind to his own beam — the studied "Brother" of feigned care contrasted with the "Thou hypocrite" of rebuke (Robertson). The hypocrisy lies in making the censure of others a cloak to seem just oneself (Theophylact). The remedy is not to abandon correction but to reverse the order: cast out your own beam first, and then you will see clearly to help your brother. Self-correction qualifies one to correct others. The verse completes the mote-and-beam parable and the warning against censoriousness: the disciple is not forbidden to help his brother, but is required first to examine and amend himself. Only the one who has dealt honestly with his own great faults can clearly and humbly help his brother with the small ones. Hypocritical censure is condemned; humble, self-corrected helpfulness is commended. First the beam, then the mote — self before brother.
43:
"For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit." The witnesses note the parable of the tree and its fruit — the test of character by conduct. Bede reads it against the hypocrites: "If you would have a true and unfeigned righteousness, what you set forth in words make up also in works, for the hypocrite though he pretends to be good is not good, who does evil works." Isidore Peleus guards against fatalism: "He does not exclude repentance, but a continuance in evil... being converted to virtue, will yield abundance. But what nature is to the tree, our affections are to us." Titus Bostrensis: "take not these words as an encouragement to idleness, for the tree is moved conformably to its nature, but you have the exercise of free will."

The grammatical voices note the connection. Calvin: the saying may be "a general instruction... that by the fruit our opinion of every man ought to be formed," connected to the reproof of hypocrites. Gill connects it to false teachers, who "cannot bring forth and publish corrupt notions" if good. The tree's nature determines its fruit; so the heart's character determines the conduct.

The parable the witnesses read as the test of true righteousness against hypocrisy. Words alone do not prove a good heart; the fruit of works reveals the tree. The good and evil are discerned not by profession but by what they bring forth — though (Isidore) the evil tree may yet be converted and made good.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the parable of the tree and fruit teaches that character is tested by conduct — a good tree cannot bring forth corrupt fruit, nor a corrupt tree good. It exposes the hypocrite, whose evil works belie his fair words (Bede); true righteousness is shown in works, not profession. The fathers guard against two errors: fatalism (the evil tree may be converted by repentance, Isidore) and idleness (man has free will, unlike the tree, Titus Bostrensis). The verse, connected to the warning against hypocrisy, supplies the test of the genuine: by their fruits, not their words, are good and evil men (and teachers, Gill) discerned. The heart's character determines the conduct, as the tree's nature determines its fruit. The disciple is warned that profession without practice is the corrupt fruit of a corrupt tree — and called to bring forth, from a heart renewed, the good fruit of love and obedience that proves the tree good.
44:
"For every tree is known by his own fruit. For of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes." The witnesses note the principle that the tree is known by its fruit. Gill: "Good and bad preachers are known by their doctrines... good and bad men are known by their lives and conversations: a holy life is the fruit of grace, and an evidence of it; and the wickedness that is in the heart of unregenerate men will show itself in the common course of their conversations."

The grammatical voices note Luke's distinctive form. Ellicott: "The form of the illustration differs slightly from that in St. Matthew, where the thorns are connected with grapes, and the figs with thistles. The word for bramble bush is the same as that used in the LXX for the burning bush on Sinai." Robertson notes the medical interest in the bramble: "Galen has a chapter on its medicinal uses." The principle is clear: each kind of plant yields its own kind of fruit, and a corrupt source cannot yield good fruit.

The application the witnesses read as the discernment of character and doctrine. As thorns yield no figs and brambles no grapes, so a corrupt heart yields no good works, and false teaching no good fruit. The test is invariable: the fruit reveals the tree, the conduct the character, the doctrine the teacher.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: every tree is known by its fruit — thorns yield no figs, brambles no grapes — so a corrupt heart yields no good works and false doctrine no good fruit (Gill). Luke's form differs slightly from Matthew's (Ellicott), and the medical interest appears in the bramble (Robertson). The principle is invariable: each source yields its own kind, and the fruit reveals the tree. The verse supplies the test for discerning character and doctrine: a holy life is the evidence of grace, and wickedness shows itself in the conduct, as the tree's nature in its fruit. The disciple is taught to judge — not censoriously (v.37) but discerningly — by the fruit: good men and true teachers by their good works and sound doctrine, evil men and false teachers by their corrupt fruit. The test cuts through profession to reality: as no thorn bears a fig, so no corrupt heart bears the good fruit of grace. By their fruits they are known.
45:
"A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh." The witnesses note the heart as the treasure from which conduct and speech flow. Cyril (under v.43): the good man "brings forth the best fruits, loving his enemy... but he who keeps a bad treasure in his heart does the contrary." Bede: "The treasure of the heart is the same as the root of the tree." Chrysostom: "when wickedness abounds within, wicked words are breathed as far as the mouth... do not suppose that there lies only so much wickedness in him as is expressed in his words, but believe the fountain to be more copious than the stream."

The grammatical voices note the relation of heart and speech. Robertson: "When men are natural, heart and mouth act in concert. But otherwise the mouth sometimes professes what the heart does not feel" (Plummer). Ellicott connects it to the judging theme: out of the good treasure the good man brings "kindness, gentleness, compassion," out of the evil treasure "bitterness, and harsh surmises, and uncharitable condemnation."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as the test of doctrine and the warning about the tongue: "the last clause has a more extensive reference than to false prophets: for it is a common proverb... we may justly and appropriately call the tongue the index of the mind" — though hypocrites may speak otherwise than they feel.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the heart is the treasure from which conduct and speech flow — the good man bringing forth good, the evil man evil, "for of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh." The heart is the root of the tree (Bede); the speech is the index of the mind (Calvin), the stream that reveals the fountain (Chrysostom) — though the fountain is more copious than the stream, and hypocrites may mask their hearts (Robertson). Ellicott connects it to the judging theme: the good heart brings forth kindness and compassion, the evil heart bitterness and uncharitable condemnation. The verse displays the inward source of all conduct and speech: the heart's treasure determines the fruit. The disciple is searched to the root: not outward profession but the heart's hidden store is the source of his words and works. To bring forth good, the heart must be made good; and the speech of the mouth, in the end, betrays the treasure of the heart. The tongue reveals the man.
46:
"And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?" The witnesses note Christ's rebuke of empty profession — the transition to the closing parable. Bede: "Lest any one should flatter himself with the words, Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks, as if words only and not rather works were required of a Christian... Why do you boast of sending forth the leaves of a right confession, and show forth no fruit of good works." Robertson: "This is the point about every sermon that counts."

The fathers note the lordship confessed. Bede: "Lordship both in name and reality belongs only to the Highest Nature." Athanasius: "This is not the word of man, but the Word of God, manifesting His own birth from the Father, for He is the Lord Who is born of the Lord alone." To call Christ "Lord" is to confess his deity — but the confession is empty without obedience.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the lesson: "God judges of the heart, not by words, but by works. A good servant never disputes, speaks little, and always follows his work... The tongue is fitly compared to a pump, which empties the heart, but neither fills nor cleanses it. The love of God is a hidden spring, which supplies the heart continually." Wesley: "What will fair professions avail, without a life answerable thereto?"

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ rebukes the empty profession that calls him "Lord, Lord" but does not obey — the leaves of confession without the fruit of works (Bede), the point on which every sermon turns (Robertson). To call Christ "Lord" is to confess his deity (Athanasius), but the confession is worthless without obedience: God judges by works, not words (Clarke), and fair professions avail nothing without a life to match (Wesley). The verse transitions to the closing parable (vv.47-49): the difference between hearing and doing. Profession alone — even the true confession of Christ's lordship — is empty leaves without fruit. The disciple is searched: do you do what Christ says, or only call him Lord? The verse cuts through all hollow religion to the test of obedience: the one who truly owns Christ as Lord will do his words. To say "Lord, Lord" while disobeying is self-deception; the proof of discipleship is not the confession of the lips but the obedience of the life.
47:
"Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them, I will shew you to whom he is like." The witnesses note the threefold mark of the true disciple — coming, hearing, doing. Gill: "Whosoever cometh to me — to be a disciple and follower." Robertson: "Hears and does — present active participles." The true disciple is marked not by coming and hearing alone but by doing; the three are joined.

On "I will shew you," Clarke: "I will show you plainly. I will enable you fully to comprehend my meaning by the following parable." Christ promises a clear illustration of the difference between the hearer who does and the hearer who does not (vv.48-49).

The three marks the witnesses read as the anatomy of true discipleship. Coming to Christ (faith), hearing his sayings (instruction), and doing them (obedience) — all three are necessary. To come and hear without doing is the empty profession of v.46; the genuine disciple adds obedience to faith and hearing.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, which introduces the closing parable. The witnesses agree: the true disciple is marked by three joined acts — coming to Christ (faith), hearing his sayings (instruction), and doing them (obedience). Hearing and doing are present participles (Robertson): the continual practice, not a single act. Christ promises to show plainly, by the parable to follow (vv.48-49), the difference this doing makes. The verse supplies the anatomy of genuine discipleship against the empty profession of v.46: it is not enough to come and hear; one must do. The one who comes, hears, and does is the wise builder on the rock (v.48); the one who hears and does not is the foolish builder on the sand (v.49). The verse sets the standard of the true follower: faith that comes, attention that hears, and obedience that does. To come and hear without doing is to build without a foundation; the disciple who would stand in the storm must add the doing of Christ's words to the hearing of them. Hearing alone saves no one; doing proves the disciple genuine.
48:
"He is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock." The witnesses note the parable of the wise builder on the rock. The fathers read the rock and the digging. Cyril: "the rock is Christ. He digs deep; by the precepts of humility He plucks out all earthly things from the hearts of the faithful." Basil: "lay your foundations upon a rock, that is, lean upon the faith of Christ, so as to persevere immovable in adversity." Ambrose: "He teaches that obedience to heavenly precepts is the foundation of all virtue, by means of which this our house can be moved neither by the torrent of pleasures, nor by the violence of spiritual wickedness, neither by the storms of this world, nor by the cloudy disputations of heretics."

The grammatical voices note Luke's distinctive detail. Robertson: "Digged and went deep — two first aorist indicatives. This wise builder struck the rock before he laid the foundation. That is the whole point." Ellicott notes the better text: "because it had been well built" — the result of digging deep to the rock was that the house was "not even shaken."

The parable the witnesses read as the doer of Christ's words (v.47). The one who hears and does has dug deep and built on the rock (Christ, faith, obedience); when the flood of trial and judgment comes, his house stands unshaken. The digging deep is the labour of true obedience that reaches the solid foundation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the doer of Christ's words is the wise builder who dug deep and laid his foundation on the rock — Christ himself (Cyril), the faith of Christ (Basil), obedience to his precepts (Ambrose). The digging deep, Luke's distinctive detail (Robertson), is the whole point: the wise builder struck the rock before building. When the flood of trial, temptation, and final judgment beats on the house, it cannot be shaken, "because it had been well built" (Ellicott). The parable illustrates the disciple who hears and does (v.47): his life, founded on Christ and built by obedience, stands firm against every storm. The verse displays the security of the true disciple: the labour of deep obedience reaches the solid rock, and the house founded there endures. Hearing and doing Christ's words is the deep foundation; and the house so built — the life of genuine, obedient faith — cannot be shaken by the floods of adversity or the storm of judgment. Built on the rock, it stands.
49:
"But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great." The witnesses note the parable of the foolish builder — the closing warning of the Sermon. Gill: "Hears Christ's sayings externally, but does not obey his commands." Chrysostom (under v.46 area): "faith profits a man nothing, if his manner of life be corrupt." Bede reads the foundationless house: "sin has no foundation, standing not by its own nature... By the force of the stream may be understood the trial of the last judgment, when both houses being finished, the wicked shall go into everlasting punishment, but the righteous into life eternal."

The grammatical voices note Luke's distinctive form. Ellicott: "More specific than St. Matthew in adding without a foundation, somewhat less so in giving on the earth instead of on the sand." The contrast with v.48 is sharp: the wise builder dug deep to the rock; the foolish built on the surface without foundation, and his house fell at once.

The "great ruin" the witnesses read as the fate of the mere hearer. Clarke (citing Quesnel): "Without a holy heart and a holy life, all is ruinous in the hour of temptation, and in the day of wrath." The hearer who does not obey, however correct his profession, builds without foundation; and when the storm of trial and judgment comes, his house falls, and great is the fall.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the Sermon. The witnesses agree: the mere hearer who does not obey is the foolish builder without a foundation, whose house falls at once in the flood — and great is the ruin. The contrast with the wise builder (v.48) is sharp: deep digging to the rock versus building on the surface. The flood is the trial of temptation and the final judgment (Bede), in which the foundationless house — the life of profession without obedience — collapses utterly. Faith profits nothing if the life is corrupt (Chrysostom); without a holy heart and life, all is ruinous in the day of wrath (Clarke). The verse closes the Sermon with a solemn warning: to hear Christ's words without doing them is to build for ruin. The whole Sermon comes to this test — not hearing but doing, not profession but obedience. The two builders divide all hearers: the doer stands, the mere hearer falls. And the fall of the foundationless house, when the storm of judgment comes, is great. Christ's last word in the Sermon is a call to build life on the rock of obedient faith, lest it perish in the day of trial.
